origin.capability_match: make important decisions on core functions of Proof-of-Liquidity & other core dApps

security_gap.gap_description: A malicious actor could submit a proposal impersonating a legitimate $BGT token holder if there isn't sufficient authentication/authorization.

actor_risk.description: External user with the ability to influence governance.

behavioral_risk.rationale: The ability to create governance proposals can be abused if not properly secured, potentially leading to malicious proposals.

assumptions: The governance contract does not have sufficient checks to prevent spoofing of proposal origin.
Proposal submission does not require strong authentication.

mitigation.strategy: Implement strong authentication mechanisms for proposal submissions, such as requiring signatures from the proposer's wallet.

threats.description: A malicious actor could spoof a legitimate $BGT token holder and submit a harmful governance proposal.

trace_source.matched_capability: make important decisions on core functions of Proof-of-Liquidity & other core dApps

origin.capability_match: cast their votes during the voting period

security_gap.gap_description: Votes could be intercepted and altered before being recorded, skewing the outcome of a governance decision.

actor_risk.description: External user with the ability to influence governance decisions.

behavioral_risk.rationale: Vote tampering can significantly impact the governance process, but the risk is mitigated by integrity requirements.

assumptions: The voting process is vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks.
There is no end-to-end encryption or signature verification for votes.

mitigation.strategy: Implement end-to-end encryption and signature verification to ensure the integrity of votes.

threats.description: A malicious actor could intercept and tamper with votes, changing the outcome of governance proposals.

trace_source.matched_capability: cast their votes during the voting period

security_gap.gap_description: Malicious users could deposit manipulated collateral to mint $HONEY.

actor_risk.description: User interacting with HoneyFactory to mint $HONEY

behavioral_risk.rationale: Tampering with collateral data could lead to minting $HONEY with less valuable assets.

assumptions: HoneyFactory does not have sufficient validation of collateral value.
The collateral price feed is vulnerable to manipulation.

mitigation.strategy: Implement robust collateral price oracles and validation mechanisms.

threats.description: A malicious user manipulates the value of the collateral asset before depositing it into the HoneyFactory, allowing them to mint more $HONEY than they are entitled to.

security_gap.gap_description: Collateral information stored on-chain can be viewed by anyone.

actor_risk.description: User interacting with HoneyFactory to mint $HONEY

assumptions: Collateral details are stored unencrypted on-chain.
Users value the privacy of their collateral holdings.

mitigation.strategy: Consider using privacy-enhancing technologies or off-chain storage for collateral details.

threats.description: An attacker monitors on-chain data to determine the collateral assets held by users in the HoneyFactory, potentially revealing sensitive financial information.

security_gap.gap_description: The HoneyFactory may be subject to a denial-of-service attack by flooding it with invalid collateral deposits.

actor_risk.description: User interacting with HoneyFactory to mint $HONEY

behavioral_risk.rationale: A large number of invalid requests can slow down or halt the HoneyFactory's ability to process legitimate transactions.

assumptions: The HoneyFactory does not have rate limiting or other DoS protection mechanisms in place.
The network can be congested by spam transactions.

mitigation.strategy: Implement rate limiting, transaction filtering, or other DoS prevention measures in the HoneyFactory contract.

threats.description: An attacker floods the HoneyFactory with a large number of invalid collateral deposit requests, causing a denial of service for legitimate users.

security_gap.gap_description: User can spoof the burn call for $BGT for $BERA

behavioral_risk.rationale: A user may spoof an authorization to burn the contract and call a different contract.

assumptions: No user input to specify who is receiving $BERA.

mitigation.strategy: Check for signature of original caller.

threats.description: User calls a separate smart contract that initiates the burning of their $BGT. The attacker could initiate a separate smart contract that calls the burn function on the $BGT but sends the $BERA to a different user.

security_gap.gap_description: The amount of $BGT used to boost a validator can be tampered with.

actor_risk.description: Any user that boosts a validator with $BGT

behavioral_risk.rationale: Tampering with the $BGT value can lead to incorrect validator boost calculation.

assumptions: There is no proper validation on the amount of $BGT being sent to the validator.
The BeraHub component does not have input sanitization for the $BGT amount.

mitigation.strategy: Implement input validation and sanitization on the BeraHub component to verify the amount of $BGT used for boosting.

threats.description: An attacker can tamper with the amount of $BGT used to boost a validator, leading to an incorrect boost calculation and potentially affecting the validator's rewards.

security_gap.gap_description: An attacker can spoof the origin of the $BGT being used to boost a validator.

actor_risk.description: Any user that boosts a validator with $BGT

behavioral_risk.rationale: Spoofing the origin of the $BGT can lead to reputation damage or Sybil attacks.

assumptions: The system does not properly authenticate the origin of the $BGT being used to boost a validator.
The BeraHub component does not have origin verification for the $BGT boost transaction.

mitigation.strategy: Implement origin authentication for $BGT boost transactions on the BeraHub component. Use signatures to verify the origin.

threats.description: An attacker can spoof the origin of the $BGT being used to boost a validator, potentially leading to Sybil attacks or reputation damage.

security_gap.gap_description: An attacker can spam the boost validator functionality, causing a denial of service.

actor_risk.description: Any user that boosts a validator with $BGT

behavioral_risk.rationale: Spamming the boost functionality can disrupt the normal operation of the validator boost system.

assumptions: There are no rate limits on the number of boost transactions a user can send.
The BeraHub component does not have rate limiting for the boost validator functionality.

mitigation.strategy: Implement rate limiting on the BeraHub component to prevent users from spamming the boost validator functionality.

threats.description: An attacker can spam the boost validator functionality, causing a denial of service and disrupting the normal operation of the validator boost system.

security_gap.gap_description: Compromised validator can direct $BGT emissions to attacker-controlled Reward Vaults

actor_risk.description: The validator, if compromised, can manipulate $BGT emissions.

behavioral_risk.rationale: Malicious validator behavior can significantly impact $BGT distribution.

assumptions: Validator's private key is compromised.
Reward Vault selection logic is vulnerable to manipulation.

mitigation.strategy: Implement robust validator key management and intrusion detection systems. Regularly audit Reward Vault selection logic.

threats.description: An attacker who compromises a validator can maliciously direct $BGT emissions to a fraudulent or attacker-controlled Reward Vault, thereby stealing $BGT intended for legitimate liquidity providers.

security_gap.gap_description: Validator can halt $BGT emissions by failing to direct emissions

actor_risk.description: Validator is responsible for directing emissions and is a single point of failure.

behavioral_risk.rationale: A validator can temporarily or permanently halt $BGT emissions.

assumptions: Validator intentionally or unintentionally stops directing $BGT emissions.
No automated fallback mechanism exists to redistribute the $BGT in such a scenario.

mitigation.strategy: Implement monitoring for validator activity and automated mechanisms to reassign $BGT emissions if a validator is non-responsive.

threats.description: A validator may, either intentionally or due to technical issues, fail to direct $BGT emissions to Reward Vaults, resulting in a temporary or permanent denial of service for liquidity providers.

security_gap.gap_description: A malicious actor may spoof a valid validator identity to maliciously direct $BGT emissions.

actor_risk.description: A malicious validator can compromise the integrity of BGT distribution.

behavioral_risk.rationale: If a validator's identity can be spoofed, the system loses control over emissions.

assumptions: The validator key management is weak, leading to unauthorized use.
The on-chain validation of validator actions is insufficient.

mitigation.strategy: Strengthen validator key management and multi-factor authentication. Implement on-chain checks to prevent unauthorized validator actions.

threats.description: An attacker who gains control over a validator's identity can direct $BGT emissions to attacker-controlled reward vaults. This undermines the Proof-of-Liquidity model.

security_gap.gap_description: Malicious actor tampers with the delegation amount before it reaches BeraHub.

behavioral_risk.rationale: Delegation tampered with by an external user could lead to incorrect validator boost.

assumptions: There is no sufficient input validation for delegation amounts.
Delegation transaction is mutable before confirmation.

mitigation.strategy: Implement client-side and server-side validation for delegation amounts. Use non-mutable transaction patterns.

threats.description: A malicious actor could intercept and tamper with the delegation amount before it reaches the BeraHub, potentially manipulating the validator's boost.

security_gap.gap_description: A malicious actor spoofs delegation source identity.

behavioral_risk.rationale: A malicious actor spoofs delegation source identity to undermine trust.

assumptions: There is no sufficient identity validation for delegation transactions.
The system does not verify the source of delegation messages.

mitigation.strategy: Implement strong identity validation for delegation transactions. Ensure source is cryptographically verified.

threats.description: A malicious actor could impersonate a legitimate BGT holder and delegate tokens on their behalf, potentially manipulating validator boost and compromising the governance process.

security_gap.gap_description: The Incentive Tokens offered by the Token Manager may be tampered with, leading to incorrect reward distribution.

actor_risk.description: One wallet address is responsible for offering an Incentive Token

behavioral_risk.rationale: Offering Incentive Tokens is a standard function, but tampering with the tokens could disrupt the reward system.

assumptions: The Token Manager's wallet is compromised, allowing an attacker to modify the Incentive Tokens.
The Incentive Token contract has a vulnerability that allows manipulation of the token balance.

mitigation.strategy: Implement strict access control and multi-factor authentication for the Token Manager's wallet. Regularly audit Incentive Token contracts for vulnerabilities.

threats.description: An attacker could tamper with the Incentive Tokens offered by the Token Manager, causing a disruption in the reward vault incentives.

security_gap.gap_description: An attacker may impersonate the Token Manager and offer malicious or valueless Incentive Tokens.

actor_risk.description: One wallet address is responsible for offering an Incentive Token

behavioral_risk.rationale: Spoofing the Token Manager is possible if the wallet is compromised, leading to the distribution of fraudulent Incentive Tokens.

assumptions: The Token Manager's private key is compromised.
There is no proper authentication mechanism to verify the identity of the Token Manager.

mitigation.strategy: Implement multi-factor authentication and strong key management practices for the Token Manager's wallet. Introduce a mechanism to verify the authenticity of the Token Manager's actions.

threats.description: An attacker may spoof the Token Manager and offer fraudulent Incentive Tokens, potentially harming the Reward Vault and its users.

security_gap.gap_description: Malicious Token Manager may intentionally halt the offering of Incentive Tokens, causing a denial of service for the Reward Vault users.

actor_risk.description: One wallet address is responsible for offering an Incentive Token

behavioral_risk.rationale: A malicious Token Manager could disrupt the system by halting the offering of Incentive Tokens, leading to a denial of service for Reward Vault users.

assumptions: The Token Manager is a trusted entity that will always act in the best interest of the system.
There is no backup mechanism to continue offering Incentive Tokens if the Token Manager becomes unavailable or malicious.

mitigation.strategy: Implement a multi-signature scheme for the Token Manager's wallet to prevent a single point of failure. Introduce a governance mechanism to replace a malicious or unavailable Token Manager.

threats.description: A malicious or unavailable Token Manager could halt the offering of Incentive Tokens, causing a denial of service for users of the Reward Vault.
